conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you some questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have one child, Alex, who's 19 and still living at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Is Alex currently in full-time approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in full-time approved education. He's doing his A-levels at the local college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful, thank you. When did Alex turn 19, or when will Alex turn 19? I need to understand if Alex is under 20 years old and whether the education started before they turned 19.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex turned 19 a few months ago, and he started the A-levels before his 19th birthday - he's been at college since last September when he was still 18.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to ask about your circumstances:

Are you currently responsible for Alex? By this I mean, does Alex live with you, and are you the person primarily responsible for their care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex does live with me, yes. But I should mention that my ex-partner also claims Child Benefit for Alex and lives with us in the same household.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that important detail. I need to understand this situation better.

To clarify: you, your ex-partner, and Alex all live together in the same household, and your ex-partner is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. We all live in the same house together, and my ex-partner is the one who currently claims the Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I have one more important question:

Are you and your ex-partner living together as a couple, or are you living in the same house but not as a couple (for example, as separate individuals or housemates)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We're not living together as a couple anymore - we're just sharing the same house but living as separate individuals.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me, I can now make a determination about your eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

Unfortunately, **you are not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

Here's why:

**About Alex's eligibility as a child:**
- Alex does meet the criteria as an eligible child because he is 19 years old, in full-time approved education (A-levels), and started that education before turning 19. Child Benefit can continue until age 20 or until the course finishes for young people in this situation.

**About your eligibility to claim:**
- However, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at any given time
- Your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Even though you're no longer living as a couple, since there is already an active claim for Alex, you cannot make a separate claim for the same child

If circumstances change in the future (for example, if your ex-partner stops claiming), you may be able to claim at that point. Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 19 years old and in full-time approved education (A-levels) that started before turning 19, which means Alex qualifies as an eligible child until turning 20 or finishing the course (whichever comes first). However, according to UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child. Even though you and your ex-partner are no longer living as a couple, you both live in the same household with Alex, and your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Since Child Benefit is already being claimed for Alex by another person in the same household, you are not eligible to also claim Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at any given time.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 19), who qualifies as an eligible child due to being in full-time approved education that started before age 19. However, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because their ex-partner is already claiming it for the same child. Under Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim for each child, and the ex-partner's existing claim takes precedence.
